cataloging_subject.label: Lollard Sermons
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the eighteen-text selection established in Gloria Cigman's EETS edition and represented by CME90174: sermons 1–16, an optional expansion designated 11A, and the Sermon of Dead Men. It is assembled from three manuscript witnesses and is not identical with any one codex. The cataloged work excludes the physical manuscripts, Cigman's 1989 edition and apparatus, the incomplete Oxford Text Archive deposit, the expanded 2023 U-M encoding, and the checked-in repository XML as distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Lollard Sermons
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous sermon writer or writers
catalog_summary.date: Probably late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; OTA catalogs 1381–1413, while the three surviving witnesses are dated to the early fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; the language has been classified as South Central Midland or Central Midlands Standard, but the exact place of composition is unknown

titles.supplied_by: Cigman's edition and subsequent cataloging tradition

assertion.notes: This is the established editorial title. The adjective 'Lollard' preserves a scholarly naming tradition; it does not resolve the disputed degree of Wycliffite affiliation.

titles.value: Lollard sermons / edited by Gloria Cigman

agents.name: Gloria Cigman
notes: Editor of the 1989 EETS edition and compiler or depositor of the OTA text; not the medieval author.

agents.name: Jeremy Griffiths
notes: Contributed the manuscript description to Cigman's edition.

agents.name: Jeremy Smith
notes: Contributed the language analysis to Cigman's edition.

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is established. Differences among the texts have prompted suggestions of more than one writer, but this record does not convert that suggestion into separate author identities.

scope.description: Manuscript description in the edition's introduction
Language analysis in the edition's introduction

assertion.notes: OTA supplies this range without an argument. Pink regards the dating as uncertain and argues that composition in the last years of the fourteenth century is possible.

date_statements.display: First decades of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: This is witness production, not a composition date; the current British Library record contains mutually inconsistent dating metadata for Add MS 41321.

places.label: South Central Midlands
notes: Used here as a linguistic classification, not as a proven place of composition.

scope.description: Language represented across the edited selection

assertion.notes: Kikusawa reports Smith's classification as South Central Midland and, more specifically, Central Midlands Standard. Manuscript scribal localization must not be treated automatically as authorial geography.

scope.description: Biblical lemmata, citations, and short formulas embedded in the Middle English sermons

form_statements.description: The collection is predominantly prose, but the official British Library description reports considerable passages of alliterative verse in sermons 8–10, copied and encoded as prose.

assertion.notes: The publisher synopsis describes the Sermon of Dead Men as a funeral sermon, while CME90174 encodes it as a treatise and the Bodleian catalog describes it as a treatise on the four last things; the genre label is therefore supported but not treated as an uncontested formal classification.

subject_statements.label: Preaching and the authority of the preached word

assertion.notes: The traditional title and some scholarship associate the texts with Lollardy, but their Wycliffite affiliation is contested and the sermons do not straightforwardly articulate every condemned Wycliffite position.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English collection of scripturally grounded sermons emphasizing preaching, moral instruction, and vernacular exegesis. Seventeen numbered gospel-sermon units follow lections for days from Advent through Lent, including an optional expansion to sermon 11 and an alternative to sermon 14; the separate Sermon of Dead Men treats the four last things. The received title associates the texts with Lollardy, but the strength and character of their Wycliffite affiliation remain disputed.

Eighteen encoded DIV1 units: sermons 1–16, optional expansion 11A, and the Sermon of Dead Men. Sermons 1–11A are based on British Library Add MS 41321; sermons 12–16 on Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 751; and the Sermon of Dead Men on Rawl. C. 751 with selected passages based on Manchester English MS 412.

The 18-text editorial unit is cataloged as an edited selection because no single manuscript contains the complete sequence represented by CME90174. Witness dates, edition dates, and encoding dates are kept separate from composition dating.

extent.description: Eighteen top-level editorial/XML units: sixteen numbered sermons plus the separately encoded optional expansion 11A (seventeen sermon-cycle units in all), and the Sermon of Dead Men. Scholarship may instead count seventeen writings by treating 11A as an expansion rather than an independent work. Sermons 8–10 contain substantial alliterative-verse passages according to the British Library description, although the manuscript and XML present them as prose; verse-line counts are therefore not asserted.

locators.value: whole work 50r–72r; nine discontinuous CME base-text milestones at 50v, 51r, 51v, 52r, 56r, 56v, 57r, 57v, and 58v

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME90174 XML header and text
evidence.summary: Identifies the 2023 U-M encoding, OTA 0174 and Cigman's edition; documents the source defects and 2023 repairs; names the three manuscript sigla; supplies keywords, titles, structural headings, and line and folio milestones.

evidence.citation: Local structural inspection of CME90174 at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.summary: Counted 18 DIV1, 811 P, 1,712 line milestones, and 350 folio milestones: 213 for A, 128 for R, and 9 for M. The milestones establish inline witness spans; for Add MS 41321, BL child-record incipit folios additionally recover opening words on shared boundary surfaces before several first inline milestones.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, Lollard sermons, ota:0174
evidence.summary: Official item record supplies the title, Middle English language, creation range 1381–1413, legacy identifier, public files, and CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license. The deposited readme and text document an incomplete source package relative to CME90174.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 48525, Lollard Sermons
evidence.summary: Catalogs Gloria Cigman's 1989 EETS Original Series 294 edition, its eighteen texts, manuscript-description and language contributors, three manuscript witnesses, publication data, pagination, and ISBN.

evidence.citation: Oxford University Press, Lollard Sermons, ISBN 9780197222966
evidence.summary: The publisher synopsis identifies the selection's gospel sermons, optional expansion, and Sermon of Dead Men and describes the last as a funeral sermon with a discourse on the four last things.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Add MS 41321/1-12
evidence.summary: Describes the gospel sermons, optional extension to sermon 11, manuscript extent and hands, Lollard sympathies, and substantial alliterative-verse passages in sermons 8–10 copied as prose. Its hierarchy and child records supply the opening folios used to restore shared boundary surfaces in the content-part ranges; its narrative and machine-readable date fields conflict and are preserved as an unresolved issue.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Archives and Manuscripts, MS. Rawl. C. 751
evidence.summary: Official record identifies the early-fifteenth-century English manuscript as containing a treatise on the four last things, sermons, and part of Poor Caitiff and confirms the current shelfmark and requestable status.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Digital Collections, English MS 412 (Pore Caitif)
evidence.summary: Official item record identifies a fifteenth-century English Middle English parchment codex and locates '[M]emorare nouissima'—the Sermon of Dead Men—at fols 50r–72r; the complete 199-canvas viewer permits exact association with canvases 138–182.

evidence.citation: Stephen Pink, Holy Scripture and the Meanings of the Eucharist in Late Medieval England, c. 1370–1430 (DPhil thesis, University of Oxford, 2011), pp. 128–133 and n. 462
evidence.summary: Treats the collection's Wycliffite affiliation as unusually disputed, reports the three manuscripts as products of the first decades of the fifteenth century, and argues that composition in the last years of the fourteenth century remains plausible.

evidence.citation: Namiko Kikusawa, Conditional if-clauses in Late Middle English prose texts (MPhil thesis, University of Glasgow, 2011), pp. 15–16
evidence.summary: Summarizes Cigman and Smith: eighteen texts, gospel lections for fifteen Sarum-calendar days, an oral-performance and preacher-guidance function, and South Central Midland or Central Midlands Standard language.

open_questions.question: What composition range can be defended for the individual sermons rather than inherited from OTA cataloging or inferred from witness dates?
next_steps: Consult Cigman's full introduction and the chronology arguments it cites.
Review post-2011 scholarship on the collection's dating and internal stratification.

open_questions.question: How should the ideological relationship of each sermon to Wycliffism or Lollardy be classified?
next_steps: Consult Cigman's introduction, Patrick Hornbeck's 2006 article, and later sermon-by-sermon analyses.
Retain the title-tradition label separately from subject assertions.

open_questions.question: Do the eighteen texts represent one anonymous author, multiple authors, or later compilation and adaptation?
next_steps: Inspect Cigman's textual and stylistic analysis and subsequent authorship scholarship.
Avoid assigning one named or collective author until the evidence distinguishes textual unity from editorial grouping.

notes: CME90174's HEADER places Cigman and the three manuscripts in AUTHOR elements; those mixed header fields are not accepted as medieval authorship statements.
The MED-stencil dates in the XML body are cataloging labels for witness-based text segments, not direct proof of composition dates.

The Add MS 41321 content-part ranges use cataloged incipit folios as well as XML milestones. Where two adjacent units share a manuscript surface, that boundary folio is intentionally present in both ranges; the first inline milestone can fall after a unit's opening words.

The record counts eighteen editorial/XML units. A scholarly count of seventeen writings is also compatible with the evidence when optional expansion 11A is treated as part of sermon 11 rather than as an independent work.
The word 'Lollard' is preserved for discovery and title fidelity while the qualified subject assertion and open question prevent it from functioning as an unexamined attribution.
